title: A Multimodal Automated Interpretability Agent
abstract: This paper describes MAIA, a Multimodal Automated Interpretability Agent. MAIA is a system that uses neural models to automate neural model understanding tasks like feature interpretation and failure mode discovery. It equips a pre-trained vision-language model with a set of tools that support iterative experimentation on subcomponents of other models to explain their behavior. These include tools commonly used by human interpretability researchers: for synthesizing and editing inputs, computing maximally activating exemplars from real-world datasets, and summarizing and describing experimental results. Interpretability experiments proposed by MAIA compose these tools to describe and explain system behavior. We evaluate applications of MAIA to computer vision models. We first characterize MAIA’s ability to describe (neuron-level) features in learned representations of images. Across several trained models and a novel dataset of synthetic vision neurons with paired ground-truth descriptions, MAIA produces descriptions comparable to those generated by expert human experimenters. We then show that MAIA can aid in two additional interpretability tasks: reducing sensitivity to spurious features, and automatically identifying inputs likely to be mis-classified.†
impact: As AI systems take on higher-stakes roles and become more deeply integrated into research and society, scalable approaches to auditing for reliability will be vital. MAIA is a protoype for a tool that can help human users ensure AI systems are transparent, reliable, and equitable. We think MAIA augments, but does not replace, human oversight of AI systems. MAIA still requires human supervision to catch mistakes such as confirmation bias and image generation/editing failures. Absence of evidence (from MAIA) is not evidence of absence: though MAIA’s toolkit enables causal interventions on inputs in order to evaluate system behavior, MAIA’s explanations do not provide formal verification of system performance. Acknowlegements We are grateful for the support of the MIT-IBM Watson AI Lab, the Open Philanthropy foundation, Hyundai Motor Company, ARL grant W911NF-18-2-021, Intel, the National Science Foundation under grant CCF-2217064, the Zuckerman STEM Leadership Program, and the Viterbi Fellowship. The funders had no role in experimental design or analysis, decision to publish, or preparation of the manuscript. The authors have no competing interests to report.

query_input.issue: Agent's feature interpretations miss that model relies on background artifacts, not biological signals.
hazard: Automated interpretability agents produce plausible but unfaithful model explanations, misleading users about true model behavior.
exposure: Biologists using agent to audit cell-classification models

hipporag_result.vulnerability: Biologists lack independent verification mechanisms, trusting the agent's plausible explanations despite their unfaithfulness to biological signals.
hipporag_result.impact: Flawed biological discoveries propagate from artifact-reliant models, corrupting scientific knowledge and downstream medical applications.
hipporag_result.dose_response: Automated interpretability agents’ plausible but unfaithful explanations, through biologists auditing cell-classification models, trigger uncritical trust without independent verification, leading to artifact-reliant models that corrupt biological discoveries and medical applications.

dose_response: Unfaithful explanations from interpretability agents cause biologists to trust artifact-driven models, leading to invalid research findings and wasted funding.
vulnerability: Scientists trust feature interpretations as biologically relevant, ignoring artifact-driven decisions.
impact: Flawed models produce invalid research findings, wasting time and funding.
